name: Broadcom
shortDescription: Broadcom designs custom AI chips and the networking silicon that wires them together for hyperscalers like Google, Meta and OpenAI, and sells VMware and other infrastructure software to enterprises. NASDAQ: AVGO; $75.5B LTM revenue, with AI semiconductors ~half of quarterly sales.
businessModel: Two engines. (1) Semiconductor Solutions: custom AI accelerators ('XPUs'/ASICs) co-designed with individual hyperscalers on 18-24 month cycles, the highest-bandwidth AI networking silicon (Tomahawk Ethernet switches, Jericho fabric routers), plus broadband, wireless (Apple connectivity), storage and industrial chips. (2) Infrastructure Software: VMware (acquired ~$69B, Nov 2023), being converted from perpetual licenses to high-margin VMware Cloud Foundation subscriptions, alongside mainframe (CA/Brocade) and security (Symantec). The combination is a free-cash-flow machine that funds a growing dividend and large buybacks.
marketContext: Broadcom is the picks-and-shovels winner of the AI build-out and the leading merchant alternative to Nvidia's GPU-plus-InfiniBand stack: it pairs custom hyperscaler silicon with the dominant AI Ethernet networking franchise, layered on a VMware/infrastructure-software cash cow. AI semiconductor revenue hit a record $10.8B (+143% YoY) in fiscal Q2 2026, roughly half of total revenue. The core debate is whether the AI ramp keeps accelerating toward management's >$100B FY2027 ambition — or whether, at ~24x EV/Revenue (2026-06-10 close), the stock is priced for perfection (an in-line, not raised, $56B FY2026 AI guide drove a ~13% post-earnings drop).
competitors: NVIDIA (NVDA) — merchant AI GPUs + InfiniBand (primary competitive frame)
Marvell Technology (MRVL) — closest custom-silicon & AI-networking peer
AMD (AMD) — merchant AI GPUs (Instinct) + data-center CPUs
customers: Hyperscaler custom-silicon customers: Google (TPU), Meta (MTIA), plus Anthropic and OpenAI among six core XPU customers
Cloud and enterprise networking customers (Tomahawk / Jericho)
Global 2000 enterprises on VMware Cloud Foundation, mainframe and Symantec

leadership.name: Hock E. Tan
leadership.title: President & Chief Executive Officer
leadership.background: Long-time CEO and architect of Broadcom's roll-up M&A strategy (Brocade, CA, Symantec, VMware) and the pivot into AI custom silicon.

leadership.name: Kirsten M. Spears
leadership.title: Chief Financial Officer (retiring June 12, 2026)
leadership.background: CFO who signed the Q2 FY2026 results after ~12 years in Broadcom finance; retiring June 12, 2026, with consulting support through March 2027.

leadership.name: Amie Thuener
leadership.title: Chief Financial Officer (incoming, effective June 12, 2026)
leadership.background: Appointed CFO in March 2026; previously VP, Corporate Controller & Chief Accounting Officer of Alphabet Inc. since 2018, earlier at PwC.

leadership.name: Charlie B. Kawwas, Ph.D.
leadership.title: President, Semiconductor Solutions Group
leadership.background: Leads the semiconductor business spanning AI accelerators and networking.

brief.businessOverview: Broadcom Inc. (NASDAQ: AVGO), headquartered in Palo Alto, CA and led by CEO Hock Tan, is a global semiconductor and infrastructure-software leader operating in two segments. Semiconductor Solutions (~68% of fiscal Q2 2026 revenue) spans custom AI accelerators ('XPUs'/ASICs) co-designed with hyperscalers, AI networking silicon (Tomahawk Ethernet switches, Jericho fabric routers), plus broadband, wireless (Apple is a large connectivity customer), server storage and industrial chips. Infrastructure Software (~32%) is anchored by VMware — acquired for roughly $69B in November 2023, which transformed Broadcom's software mix — alongside mainframe (CA/Brocade) and security (Symantec). The company has reoriented around the AI build-out: in fiscal Q2 2026 (ended May 3, 2026), AI semiconductor revenue hit a record $10.8B (+143% YoY), about half of total revenue, with networking ~40% of AI sales. Broadcom is the dominant merchant custom-silicon (ASIC) designer, now serving six core XPU customers including Google (TPU), Meta (MTIA), Anthropic and OpenAI. It trades near $372 as of the 2026-06-10 close (~$1.8T market cap).

businessOverviewSources.name: CNBC — OpenAI partners with Broadcom on custom AI chips

brief.productModel: Broadcom's economics rest on two engines. (1) AI semiconductors: it co-designs custom XPUs/ASICs with individual hyperscalers (18-24 month design cycles) and supplies the highest-bandwidth Ethernet networking silicon (Tomahawk 6 at 102.4 Tbps; the Jericho4 fabric router links more than 1M XPUs across data centers). Management has reaffirmed roughly $56B of FY2026 AI semiconductor revenue and reiterated a more than $100B FY2027 AI-revenue ambition, with AI bookings exceeding $30B. (2) Infrastructure software: VMware is being converted from perpetual licenses to VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) subscriptions, lifting recurring revenue and margins. The model is a free-cash-flow machine — fiscal Q2 2026 free cash flow was $10.26B (46% of revenue) and adjusted EBITDA was $15.24B (69% margin) — funding a $0.65/quarter dividend (raised 10% for FY2026) and a $10B buyback authorization.

recentNews.headline: Broadcom posts record fiscal Q2 2026: revenue $22.2B (+48%), AI semis $10.8B (+143%); stock falls ~13% on unchanged $56B AI guide
recentNews.whyItMatters: Confirms AI is now ~half of revenue, but shows the stock is priced for perfection — an in-line (not raised) full-year AI guide and a small VMware/software miss triggered a double-digit selloff.

recentNews.headline: Broadcom names Alphabet finance exec Amie Thuener CFO, succeeding retiring Kirsten Spears effective June 12, 2026
recentNews.whyItMatters: First CFO change in years; hiring Alphabet's chief accounting officer adds hyperscaler-finance pedigree as AI scales, but is a key-person transition to watch.

recentNews.headline: Broadcom fiscal Q1 2026: revenue $19.3B, AI revenue $8.4B (+106%); new $10B buyback authorized
recentNews.whyItMatters: Showed AI revenue more than doubling and signaled capital-return capacity even as the company invests in the AI ramp.

recentNews.headline: Broadcom reports record FY2025: revenue $63.9B (+24%); AI sales reached $20B (+65% YoY); raises dividend 10% to $0.65
recentNews.whyItMatters: First full year with VMware fully consolidated and AI scaling — FCF of $26.9B underpins the dividend and buyback and frames the FY2026 AI ramp.

recentNews.headline: OpenAI and Broadcom announce strategic collaboration to deploy 10 GW of OpenAI-designed AI accelerators
recentNews.whyItMatters: Adds OpenAI as a marquee custom-silicon customer; production starts 2H 2026, full rollout by 2029 — materially expanding the multi-year XPU pipeline beyond Google and Meta.

earningsTakeaways.aiCommentary: CEO Hock Tan said fiscal Q2 AI semiconductor revenue of $10.8B grew 143% YoY 'above our forecast, driven by increasing demand for custom AI accelerators and AI networking,' and the company expects fiscal Q3 AI revenue to grow more than 200% YoY to $16.0B. Management reaffirmed (did not raise) roughly $56B of FY2026 AI semiconductor revenue and reiterated a more than $100B FY2027 AI-revenue ambition, with AI bookings above $30B. Six core custom-XPU customers now include Google, Meta, Anthropic and OpenAI.
earningsTakeaways.demandCommentary: Networking was ~40% of AI semiconductor revenue (Tomahawk/Jericho). Infrastructure Software ($7.18B) slightly missed the ~$7.32B Street estimate as the VMware subscription conversion matures, and the stock fell ~13% post-print because the unchanged $56B AI guide was read as a ceiling rather than a raise. The non-AI semiconductor portfolio (broadband, wireless, industrial) remains cyclical, but free cash flow of $10.26B (46% of revenue) keeps funding the dividend and buyback.

keyRisks: Customer concentration: a handful of hyperscalers (Google, Meta, plus Apple in wireless) and now OpenAI/Anthropic drive a large share of AI and total revenue — loss, in-sourcing, or a capex pause at any one would hit results outsized.

AI-revenue durability vs. Nvidia merchant GPUs: the bull case rests on hyperscalers preferring custom XPUs and Ethernet over Nvidia's GPU+InfiniBand stack; an unchanged $56B FY2026 AI guide already spooked investors who feared a ceiling.

VMware pricing backlash / churn: the forced perpetual-to-subscription conversion and large VCF bundles have driven steep price increases and customer pushback, and fiscal Q2 software revenue already slightly missed the Street.
China & geopolitical exposure: U.S.-China export controls and softer Chinese demand weigh on the non-AI semiconductor and networking businesses and add regulatory uncertainty.
Debt and integration from VMware: the ~$69B VMware deal loaded the balance sheet with debt; deleveraging competes with the dividend, buyback, and AI investment for cash.

Valuation & cyclicality: at ~24x EV/Revenue (2026-06-10 close) and a ~$1.8T market cap, the multiple prices in flawless AI execution, while the non-AI semiconductor portfolio remains cyclical and has been a recent drag.

keyRisksSources.name: CNBC — Broadcom fiscal Q2 2026 earnings / stock drop

comps.name: Broadcom
comps.note: subject; custom AI silicon + dominant AI Ethernet networking, layered on a VMware FCF cash cow; ~46% FCF margin funds a growing dividend

comps.name: NVIDIA
comps.note: the merchant-GPU incumbent and primary competitive frame; faster-growing at a lower EV/Rev, the GPU+InfiniBand stack Broadcom's custom-ASIC+Ethernet alternative competes against

comps.name: Marvell Technology
comps.note: closest custom-silicon (ASIC) and AI-networking peer; the other merchant designer chasing hyperscaler XPU and optical sockets, lower gross margin

comps.name: AMD
comps.note: merchant AI GPU (Instinct) and data-center CPU challenger; competes for the same hyperscaler AI compute budget

comps.name: Qualcomm
comps.note: diversified mobile/edge connectivity peer; far less AI-data-center levered and far cheaper — a valuation/cyclicality contrast point

swot.standoutSummary: Broadcom is the only at-scale merchant designer pairing custom AI accelerators (XPUs) AND the dominant AI Ethernet networking stack — a hyperscaler-aligned alternative to Nvidia — layered on a VMware/software cash cow that funds a sector-leading ~46% FCF margin and a growing dividend. At ~24x EV/Rev (2026-06-10 close) it sits between faster-growing Nvidia (~19x) and lower-margin Marvell (~26x).

strengths.detail: Six core XPU customers (Google, Meta, Anthropic, OpenAI + two unnamed) on 18-24 month co-design cycles create deep switching costs and >$30B of AI bookings visibility.
Tomahawk 6 (102.4 Tbps) and Jericho4 make Broadcom the de facto Ethernet-for-AI standard-bearer; ~40% of AI revenue is networking, diversifying beyond accelerators.
Fiscal Q2 2026 FCF of $10.26B (46% of revenue) and a 69% adjusted EBITDA margin; VMware is a high-margin recurring cash cow funding the dividend and $10B buyback.

weaknesses.detail: A few hyperscalers plus Apple drive an outsized revenue share; results swing on a handful of customers' capex and in-sourcing decisions.
Aggressive subscription/price resets are alienating some enterprise customers; fiscal Q2 software revenue already missed the Street estimate.
VMware-related debt competes with AI investment and capital returns for cash flow.

opportunities.detail: Management's >$100B FY2027 AI-revenue ambition and the OpenAI 10 GW deal extend the runway well beyond FY2026's ~$56B.
The industry shift toward open Ethernet for AI scale-up/scale-out plays directly to Broadcom's switch/router franchise.
Full subscription conversion lifts recurring revenue and margins as the installed base renews onto VCF.

threats.detail: Nvidia's GPU + NVLink/InfiniBand stack and pace of innovation could blunt custom-ASIC and Ethernet adoption.
~24x EV/Rev (2026-06-10 close) prices in perfection — an in-line guide already drove a ~13% drop; any AI-demand wobble de-rates the multiple fast.
Export controls and a cyclical non-AI chip portfolio (broadband, wireless, industrial) remain downside swing factors.

sources.name: yfinance / Yahoo Finance — live comps (2026-06-10 close)

slideBullets: AI is now ~half the business: record AI semiconductor revenue of $10.8B (+143% YoY) in fiscal Q2 2026 on total revenue of $22.2B (+48%), with management guiding fiscal Q3 AI revenue to $16.0B (>200% YoY).

Two-sided AI moat: Broadcom is the leading merchant designer of custom hyperscaler accelerators (XPUs for Google, Meta, Anthropic, OpenAI) AND the dominant AI Ethernet networking vendor (Tomahawk, Jericho) — ~40% of AI revenue is networking.

Cash machine: fiscal Q2 2026 free cash flow of $10.26B (46% of revenue) and a 69% adjusted EBITDA margin fund a $0.65 dividend (raised 10%) and a $10B buyback, with VMware a high-margin recurring cash cow.
The debate is the trajectory: management reaffirmed — but did not raise — ~$56B of FY2026 AI revenue and reiterated a >$100B FY2027 ambition; the unchanged guide drove a ~13% post-earnings selloff.

Priced for perfection at ~24x EV/Revenue (as of the 2026-06-10 close): bulls underwrite the AI ramp and FCF, bears point to customer concentration, VMware pricing backlash, and a cyclical non-AI chip portfolio.
diligenceQuestions: How durable and diversified is the AI custom-silicon pipeline beyond Google and Meta? What is the revenue ramp, design-win timing, and margin profile of the newer XPU programs (OpenAI's 10 GW, Anthropic, and the unnamed customers), and how exposed is the >$100B FY2027 ambition to any single hyperscaler's capex or a decision to in-source?

What is the real trajectory of VMware after the subscription conversion? What are renewal/retention rates, the magnitude of price resets, churn to alternatives (Nutanix, Red Hat, hyperscaler-native), and how much of the ~32% software mix is durable recurring revenue versus a one-time conversion bump?

How should investors frame the custom-ASIC-plus-Ethernet bet against Nvidia? Where are hyperscalers actually splitting spend between Broadcom XPUs and merchant GPUs, how is Ethernet winning vs InfiniBand in real deployments, and what would a slowdown in AI training capex do to both the accelerator and networking lines?

whatMatters.thesis: Broadcom is the picks-and-shovels winner of the AI build-out — custom accelerators plus the dominant AI networking stack — wrapped around a VMware cash cow throwing off ~$10B of quarterly free cash flow.
whatMatters.debate: Is the AI ramp accelerating or plateauing? An in-line (not raised) $56B FY2026 AI guide drove a ~13% selloff — bulls see >$100B by FY2027, bears see a stock priced for perfection at ~24x EV/Rev (2026-06-10 close).
whatMatters.catalyst: Fiscal Q3 2026 results (quarter ending ~August 2, 2026), where management guides AI semiconductor revenue to $16.0B (>200% YoY) — the next test of whether the ramp re-accelerates, and new CFO Amie Thuener's first full quarter.

plain: Broadcom makes the specialized chips that power AI data centers and the internet.
Its biggest growth: custom AI chips built for Google, Meta, OpenAI and others.
It also makes the networking gear that wires thousands of chips together.
Plus it sells VMware software that runs companies' data-center servers.
technical: Designs custom XPU/ASIC AI accelerators co-developed with each hyperscaler customer.
Supplies AI networking silicon (Tomahawk switches, Jericho routers) for Ethernet clusters.
Runs VMware/infrastructure software on a high-margin subscription (VCF) model.
Converts ~46% of revenue to free cash flow, funding the dividend and buybacks.
simple: Think of Broadcom as the company that builds custom engines AND the highways for AI.
Big AI firms hire it to design a chip tuned to exactly their needs.
And it owns VMware, the software that quietly runs corporate computer rooms.

glossary.definition: A chip custom-built for one specific job, making it faster and cheaper at that task than a general-purpose chip.
Broadcom's term for a custom AI accelerator chip it co-designs with a specific hyperscaler customer.
A chip specialized to crunch the math behind AI models far faster than a normal CPU.
A giant cloud/data-center operator (Google, Meta, Amazon, Microsoft, OpenAI) buying compute at massive scale.
The open networking standard Broadcom champions to connect many chips/servers so they act as one big computer.
A competing high-speed networking standard (associated with Nvidia) that Broadcom's Ethernet products aim to displace in AI clusters.
Software that splits one physical server into many virtual machines, letting companies run their data centers more efficiently.
The cash a business generates after operating costs and capital spending — the money available for dividends, buybacks and debt paydown.
